reason_for_request: 根据 unresolved cluster 请求 LLM 给出注释链接修补建议

request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。

      "page_no": 603,
      "ocr_excerpt": "Some of the doubts that officials expressed about the madness (or “besottedness”) of text bearers probably resulted from bureaucratic necessity. When reporting on a case, they could not assume that one who looked or sounded crazy was crazy. This “institutionalized strategy” (Bourdieu 1990: 138) on the part of officials was the object of no specific rule, but it was strongly encouraged in bureaucratic practice. Those officials who embodied this fear of mad-sounding speech were praised, whereas officials who simply assumed that the authors of these texts were mad were punished if their negligence was later discovered. $ ^{25} $ But even when officials had found no illegal activities behind a suspicious text, they were not necessarily clement. Although Feng Qiyan was eventually recognized to have suffered from “mucus confusion,” his punishment was not lenient. Feng Qiyan’s having illicitly written a petition for a trifling marriage matter and intended to present it in front of the imperial convoy was truly stupid, reckless, and illegal. unmatched_anchors.source_text: past. A flurry of articles on that subject appeared in the first year of publication of several academic or semi-academic journals in 1979 and 1980, soon after the Third Plenum of the Eleventh Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party in late 1978 had confirmed the fall of the Gang of Four and modified conditions of scholarship. $ ^{5} $

An even more recent wave of scholarship on wenziyu has by and large retained the view that the Qing indiscriminately repressed young Chinese thinkers and intellectuals. $ ^{6} $ Anecdotal accounts and emphasis on the unfairness of the condemnations are still the chief aspects of this scholarship. Now that the anti-Manchu sentiment has disappeared (since the Manchus are now retrospectively presented as a “minority nationality” within the broad Chinese nation), the fault for wenziyu is rejected on “feudal government” in general.

As many of the men I discuss in this chapter, Chai Shilu's brother Shijin 世進 came to official attention while trying to present a text to an imperial administrator. Donations of letters and texts were not always illegal, but the Qing code contained a law that forbade the presentation of texts in order to gain recognition or employment: seeking advancement under a false pretense was to be punished with 100 strokes of the heavy stick. $ ^{9} $

Despite the heavy punishment incurred, all sorts of people presented texts to officials to attract their attention. Many of the cases I discuss in this chapter commenced in a similar fashion, when “mad people” contacted the officialdom to voice grievances, to express praise for the emperor, or to seek advice or employment. $ ^{10} $ Education commissioners’ (學證) constant travels, their proximity to licentiates, and their moral reputation made them favorite targets for grievance bearers and letter writers. $ ^{11} $

Despite the heavy punishment incurred, all sorts of people presented texts to officials to attract their attention. Many of the cases I discuss in this chapter commenced in a similar fashion, when “mad people” contacted the officialdom to voice grievances, to express praise for the emperor, or to seek advice or employment. $ ^{10} $ Education commissioners’ (學證) constant travels, their proximity to licentiates, and their moral reputation made them favorite targets for grievance bearers and letter writers. $ ^{11} $

Offerings of dubious texts were matters serious enough to be discussed in palace memorials, a form of document designed for reporting on important state matters. Governors and Governors-general usually conducted interrogations in person and were responsible for mobilizing lower officials for swift and thorough investigations. Officials scrutinized the family background and network of relations of the “criminals,” their possible involvement in unlawful activities, and whether they were concealing more “illicit writings.” $ ^{12} $

Official suspicion about these texts contrasted with the hopes of those who presented them. Wang Zhaoji 王肇基 presented a poem in praise of the emperor to a Shanxi prefect in 1751, but his eulogy was found to include "recklessly seditious passages" (狂妄悖逆之處) and words that "slandered the sages" (毀謗聖賢). $ ^{13} $ As he confessed, "In offering a poem respectfully wishing ten thousand years to the Empress Dowager, I was only doing my utmost as a small commoner. All I wanted was to please His Imperial

Majesty”(我獻詩恭祝皇太后萬歲 不過盡我小民之心 欲求皇上喜歡的意思). He was sentenced to death by beating, a heavy punishment considering that officials admitted he did it “without awareness”(無知).¹⁴ The use of this irregular punishment indicates that Wang’s “crime” was considered serious enough to justify disregard for penal denominations, but that it was not completely on a par with treason or sedition, which would have led to the execution of all of Wang’s male relatives and the enslavement of his female kin.

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: For officials who were concerned with sedition (or advancement), one issue was how to prevent commoners from presenting “seditious petitions.” Chen Hongmou 陳宏謀 (1696-1771), activist official par excellence, noted in a 1763 memorial that non-violent madmen were rarely confined, because they were unlikely to commit a homicide. $ ^{15} $ Expressing his fear that some men would exploit this “weakness” of the policy of preventive confinement to foster disorder, Chen proposed to imprison as strictly as violent madmen those insane people who were meek but literate. They should also be denied access to writing implements. We know of Chen’s memorial through an appreciative edict by the Qianlong emperor: $ ^{16} $

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: past. A flurry of articles on that subject appeared in the first year of publication of several academic or semi-academic journals in 1979 and 1980, soon after the Third Plenum of the Eleventh Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party in late 1978 had confirmed the fall of the Gang of Four and modified conditions of scholarship. $ ^{5} $ An even more recent wave of scholarship on wenziyu has by and large retained the view that the Qing indiscriminately repressed young Chinese thinkers and intellectuals. $ ^{6} $ Anecdotal accounts and emphasis on the unfairness of the condemnations are still the chief aspects of this scholarship. Now that the anti-Manchu sentiment has disappeared (since the Manchus are now retrospectively presented as a “minority nationality” within the broad Chinese nation), the fault for wenziyu is rejected on “feudal government” in general. There may be issues that partisan debates and anecdotal accounts have kept us from noticing in these trials. What kinds of words or writings were considered seditious enough to deserve death even as those who uttered them were recognized as crazy? What type of political regime could feel threatened by such words? In what contexts were expressions of loyalty unacceptable? Are we being misled by what we feel is a clear boundary between madness and crime? I seek answers to these questions in four interrelated domains ...

Wang Zhaoji 王肇基 presented a poem in praise of the emperor to a Shanxi prefect in 1751, but his eulogy was found to include "recklessly seditious passages" (狂妄悖逆之處) and words that "slandered the sages" (毀謗聖賢). $ ^{13} $ As he confessed, "In offering a ...

Majesty”(我獻詩恭祝皇太后萬歲 不過盡我小民之心 欲求皇上喜歡的意思). He was sentenced to death by beating, a heavy punishment considering that officials admitted he did it “without awareness”(無知).¹⁴ The use of this irregular punishment indicates that Wang’s “crime” was considered serious enough to justify disregard for penal denominations, but that it was not completely on a par with treason or sedition, which would have led to the execution of all of Wang’s male relatives and the enslavement of his female kin. For officials who were concerned with sedition (or advancement), one issue was how to prevent commoners from presenting “seditious petitions.” Chen Hongmou 陳宏謀 (1696-1771), activist official par excellence, noted in a 1763 memorial that non-violent madmen were rarely confined, because they were unlikely to commit a homicide. $ ^{15} $ Expressing his fear that some men would exploit this “weakness” of the policy of preventive confinement to foster disorder, Chen proposed to imprison as strictly as violent madmen those insane people who were meek but literate. They should also be denied access to writing implements. We know of Chen’s memorial through an appreciative edict by the Qianlong emperor: $ ^{16} $ There is some vision in what is reported in Chen Hongmou's memorial requesting the locking up of mad people. A sub-statute was once established [that imposed] strict restrictions on mad people whos ...

truly lost their heart and become sickly mad also turn to writing and [pretend to suffer from] madness illness, hoping to bend the law. $ ^{19} $ This [matter] has to do with local mores and people's hearts. $ ^{20} $ The original memorial has been copied and sent to every Governor and Governor-general for their perusal, so that they can more easily report [on the current situation in their province?]. They have been ordered to instruct their subordinates immediately that when they encounter such people suffering from madness illness, they should take strict preventive measures of guard and precaution, and that no slackness and idleness will be allowed. 陳宏謀請鎖錮瘋人一摺 所奏亦有所見 向來此等瘋人病發 原定有嚴加約束之例 但行之日久 地方官不能實力奉行 以致旋鎖旋釋 甚至任其播弄筆墨 滋生事端 而匪人中實系喪心病狂者亦轉藉詞瘋病 冀為骫法 與風俗人心甚有關係 著於各督撫奏事之便 將原摺抄寄閱看 令其即飭所屬遇有此等瘋病之人 應預為嚴行看守防範 毋得稍有弛懈 Chen knew that the mix of madness with a brush had already led to sedition cases, but the measures he proposed were not very concrete. Indeed, as far as I know, his proposal was never implemented. His claim that some criminals pretended to be mad in order to escape punishment made little sense, since, as we shall see, men who were recognized as mad were commonly executed when they wrote seditious texts. ## Lecturing the emperor: two cases Inappropriate statements did not have to be treasonous in order to infuriate the sovereign. Yan Zeng 嚴譜, a man from Shanxi, had heard a ru ...

Chinese)—was so grieved by his conduct that she had committed suicide. $ ^{21} $ Ula Nara was born in 1718 and had become Empress in 1750. She had indeed accompanied the emperor on his fourth Southern Tour in early 1765, but for reasons that are still unclear, the monarch suddenly had ordered her to return to Beijing. She either died of grief or committed suicide in the palace the following year, after she had obviously fallen out of favor. $ ^{22} $ Yan presented his message to a Shanxi official in 1777. Written in the form of a palace memorial, it was a sympathetic reproof of the sovereign's alleged misconduct: Empress Na[la] was plain and excellent, principled and chaste, and had been blessed with much doting. Seeing that His Majesty, despite being over five-decades old and busy with numerous and complicated matters of state, still showed special favor to ladies as in the past, she was afraid this was not a good way of nurturing the Sagely imperial body. For this reason, and because she had to endure such an offense, she made light of her own life. 納皇后質美節烈 多蒙寵愛 見皇上年過五旬 國事紛繁 若仍如前寵幸 恐非善養聖體 是以故加挺觸輕生 Yan was sentenced to death by dismemberment, probably as much for his unbecoming address as for having forged a palace memorial. The case of Feng Qiyan 馮起炎 took place in Shanxi in 1783, when Feng was 31 sui. $ ^{23} $ He had been born a Zhao 趙, but when his father had died his mothe ...

second father also died of illness, leaving him and his mother without means. For years he was unable to find work or to progress further in the examination system. In 1781, however, he moved to a school attached to the military garrison of Pingyang, where he made a living copying documents (借住平陽營子識文 在茲家學堂抄寫文冊餬口). There, he kept a crumbling copy of the Four Books, an Old-Text version of the Classic of Poetry, and a copy of the well-liked Romance of the Western Chamber (Xixiang ji 西廂記). Despite having found employment, Feng grew so impoverished that he became depressed and restless (贫苦難度 憂鬱無聊) and his despondency eventually turned into an illness (抑鬱成病). $ ^{24} $ In 1782, after he caught what later reports called “mucus-confusion disorder” (痰迷之症), he hurt his legs trying to commit suicide by jumping from the Pingyang city wall. A few months later, just after the lunar New Year, he left for Beijing, planning to rejoin the Zhao line (意欲歸宗) and to study trade (學習買賣). He arrived 15 days later, carrying commentaries he had written on the Classic of Poetry and the Book of Changes. Since Feng had been a student for his entire life, his Beijing relatives urged him to abandon his commercial plans. His uncle gave him 1000 in copper cash and his younger brother 500 in Beijing currency (大錢). In an inn on the way back home, Feng Qiyan heard that the imperial carriage was coming hither to vi ...

Some of the doubts that officials expressed about the madness (or “besottedness”) of text bearers probably resulted from bureaucratic necessity. When reporting on a case, they could not assume that one who looked or sounded crazy was crazy. This “institutionalized strategy” (Bourdieu 1990: 138) on the part of officials was the object of no specific rule, but it was strongly encouraged in bureaucratic practice. Those officials who embodied this fear of mad-sounding speech were praised, whereas officials who simply assumed that the authors of these texts were mad were punished if their negligence was later discovered. $ ^{25} $ But even when officials had found no illegal activities behind a suspicious text, they were not necessarily clement. Although Feng Qiyan was eventually recognized to have suffered from “mucus confusion,” his punishment was not lenient. Feng Qiyan’s having illicitly written a petition for a trifling marriage matter and intended to present it in front of the imperial convoy was truly stupid, reckless, and illegal. Though his planning to marry someone’s daughters resembles besotted confusion, it remains that his words are clear. And how can we know that he is not cunningly pretending to be confused in order to unleash his mad barking? It would thus be unsuitable to be even slightly indulgent.
